behaviors.description: A file placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root is processed, not only files placed directly in the root directory.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is real OS filesystem watching semantics (recursive watch behavior on nested paths). A mock watcher can emit a synthetic nested-path event and pass even if real watcher configuration or platform behavior misses subdirectories. Rejected higher levels: behavior text is purely filesystem/event handling, with no AI/ML output or multi-agent sequence.
behaviors.test_description: Start the daemon against a real temporary watched root on disk, create a nested subdirectory, write a file into that subdirectory, and assert one transformed output appears in the output directory for that file without restarting the daemon.

behaviors.description: The deduplication logic correctly discards a second processing request for the same file path within a configurable time window.
behaviors.justification: This is primarily local module handoff/state behavior (request intake -> dedup cache/window -> pipeline enqueue). Minimum real failure boundary is cross-module state propagation with time-window config, which a pure unit test of one function may miss. Rejected System: behavior text says 'processing request' and does not require OS/network/subprocess conditions. Rejected Agentic/Workflow: no model-dependent decisions.
behaviors.test_description: Wire the real request-ingest and dedup modules in-process with a controllable clock, submit two requests for the same canonical path within the configured window, and assert only one enqueue/processing attempt is emitted; then advance past the window and assert a new request is accepted.

behaviors.description: When the operating system emits multiple filesystem events for a single file write, the daemon processes the file exactly once.
behaviors.justification: Behavior explicitly depends on real OS duplicate event emission patterns. In-process fakes can trivially simulate duplicates but may hide timing/coalescing quirks that cause real double-processing. Rejected higher levels: no AI/ML output dependency and no multi-agent chain in behavior text.
behaviors.test_description: Run the daemon on a real filesystem, perform write patterns known to trigger multiple events (e.g., temp-write+rename or rapid append/close), capture processing invocations, and assert exactly one pipeline run/output per logical file.

behaviors.description: If the watched directory is removed and then recreated while the daemon is running, the daemon resumes watching the new directory without a restart.
behaviors.justification: This failure appears at OS watcher lifecycle boundaries (directory deletion/recreation, watch handle invalidation/rebind). Module-only wiring cannot faithfully reproduce kernel watcher behavior. Rejected higher levels: behavior is infra resilience only, not AI/ML or multi-agent sequencing.
behaviors.test_description: Launch daemon watching a real directory, delete the watched directory while daemon remains running, recreate it at the same path, then create a new file and assert it is processed successfully without restarting the daemon process.

behaviors.description: When multiple files arrive at the same time, they are processed in the order determined by their creation timestamps.
behaviors.justification: Ordering depends on real filesystem timestamp values/resolution and concurrent arrival timing; mocks can impose idealized timestamps/order and miss real races or equal-timestamp edge cases. Rejected higher levels: no AI/ML decisions and no agent pipeline chaining in behavior text.
behaviors.test_description: With daemon running on a real filesystem, create multiple files near-simultaneously, record each file’s creation timestamp as seen by the daemon path/stat logic, and assert processing order matches the timestamp-based ordering rule (including tie-break behavior if specified).

behaviors.description: A file that is still being written to when the arrival event fires is not picked up for processing until writing is complete.
behaviors.justification: Requires real write-in-progress behavior and event timing from OS/filesystem. Stubbed events and fake file handles often hide partial-write races that occur in production. Rejected higher levels: behavior concerns filesystem readiness gating only, not AI/ML output quality or multi-agent sequencing.
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon on a real directory, open a file and write it in chunks with delays while keeping the writer open, trigger arrival event naturally via filesystem, and assert daemon does not process until final close/complete-write condition is met; then assert single successful processing after completion.
